coredata.dc:title: Development and characterization of a novel pH-Responsive nanocarrier for enhanced quercetin delivery and cytotoxicity in lung cancer
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaei Abbas Abad, Fateme
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Abdouss, Majid
coredata.dc:description: Quercetin (QC) is a potent polyphenol with numerous health benefits, but its use is limited due to its low bioavailability and high toxicity. Accordingly, a pH-responsive nanocarrier was created using chitosan (CS), halloysite nanotubes (HNTs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). It was found that LE and EE values of the CS/GQDs nanocomposites were 32.0% and 69.0%, respectively, increasing to 44.25% and 85.5% upon HNTs addition. The QC release study of pH-responsive CS/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite in buffer environments showed that with the presence of GQDs in the CS/HNTs composite, the release rate increased from 73% and 54% at pH 5.4 and 7.4 to 97.5% and 67%. Examining the release data with kinetic models showed that the data corresponded to the first-order model, which indicates concentration-dependent drug release. In this study, the cytotoxicity of CS/HNT, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposites against L929 and A549 lung cell lines was investigated. The results showed that CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC had the highest cytotoxicity and had a more profound effect on A549 cells. The proportion of apoptotic cells in the CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC group was also higher than in the control group, with early and late apoptosis of 74.4% and 5.15%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chitosan
Quercetin
Graphene quantum dots
Halloysite nanotubes
Targeted drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel and loading of QC
Determination of LE and EE of QC
Kinetics release of drug in various pH grades

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR (A) and XRD (B) spectra of CS, GQDs, HNTs, CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite.
(A) DLS and (B) ZP of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite.

FE-SEM images of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposites. Scale bar: 1
μ m
(left) and 200 nm (Right).
Cumulative curves of QC release from nanocomposite carriers.
Fitting of the drug release data to different kinetic model.
In vitro cytotoxicity of control, CS, CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC and free QC against A549 and L929 cell lines after 24 h.
Flow cytometry results of apoptosis and necrosis in free QC and prepared nanocomposites (CS/HNTs/GQDs and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC)-induced A549 cells.
A) Schematic of CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel loaded with QC, (B) Preparation steps of CS/HNTs, CS/ GQDs and CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel loaded by QC.
QC %LE and %EE of the delivery systems reported in the literature and its comparison with those estimated in this work.
Effect of HNTs on QC encapsulation and loading efficiencies.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Data is the average result of three tests under the same conditions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The parameters of kinetic models utilized to correlate drug release data.

head.ce:title: Development and characterization of a novel pH-Responsive nanocarrier for enhanced quercetin delivery and cytotoxicity in lung cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Amirkabir University of Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, GC, 1983963113, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, GC, 1983963113, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A) Schematic of CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel loaded with QC, (B) Preparation steps of CS/HNTs, CS/ GQDs and CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel loaded by QC.

Quercetin (QC) is a potent polyphenol with numerous health benefits, but its use is limited due to its low bioavailability and high toxicity. Accordingly, a pH-responsive nanocarrier was created using chitosan (CS), halloysite nanotubes (HNTs), and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). It was found that LE and EE values of the CS/GQDs nanocomposites were 32.0% and 69.0%, respectively, increasing to 44.25% and 85.5% upon HNTs addition. The QC release study of pH-responsive CS/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite in buffer environments showed that with the presence of GQDs in the CS/HNTs composite, the release rate increased from 73% and 54% at pH 5.4 and 7.4 to 97.5% and 67%. Examining the release data with kinetic models showed that the data corresponded to the first-order model, which indicates concentration-dependent drug release. In this study, the cytotoxicity of CS/HNT, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposites against L929 and A549 lung cell lines was investigated. The results showed that CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC had the highest cytotoxicity and had a more profound effect on A549 cells. The proportion of apoptotic cells in the CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC group was also higher than in the control group, with early and late apoptosis of 74.4% and 5.15%, respectively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is the leading cause of death worldwide, following heart disease and other illnesses. It is a complex genetic disease that manifests at the tissue, cellular, and tumor levels, making conventional treatment methods ineffective. Cancer cells employ the body's sophisticated mechanisms to survive and combat therapeutic agents, necessitating the development of a practical approach to halt and eliminate these cells.[1,2] Several cancer treatment approaches have been proposed, including radiation therapy, surgery, immunotherapy, chemotherapy, hyperthermia, gene therapy, and nanomedicine preparation using nanoparticles. [2,3] Chemotherapy appears to be a viable option for cancer treatment. However, one of the primary drawbacks of this method is the lack of selectivity of anti-cancer drugs, as even healthy cells are affected. [4] With advancements in science and technology, targeted drug delivery systems have been developed that utilize nanocarriers (with tiny diameters) for drug loading and encapsulation, enhancing drug delivery. [4–6] These nanocarriers can be responsive to pH, temperature, magnetic fields, and other factors, and this sensitivity can trigger the release of the drug in the tumor environment. [7] Quercetin (QC) is a polyphenolic anti-cancer drug that was first identified in herbal sources and later transitioned to industrial production. QC is a flavonoid group polyphenol found in fruits, vegetables (berries, apples, red onions, and citrus fruits), nuts, seeds, and plants. Its most notable biological activities are its anti-cancer, antioxidant, blood-fat-lowering, anti-inflammatory, and blood vessel-opening properties. [8] QC supplementation has been reported safe in numerous human and animal studies, with no adverse symptoms or harmful physiological effects. [9] Despite its therapeutic properties, QC has a very low bioavailability due to degradation by light, low solubility in water, oxygen, and heat, and low biological activity in the digestive system. Therefore, it must be introduced into the body in a protected form. Loading this drug in a nanocarrier safeguards it, enhances its bioavailability, and prevents its unintended impact on healthy cells. [10].

Chitosan (CS) is one of the natural hydrophilic and cationic polymers commonly used to prepare drug delivery systems. Glucosamine and N-acetyl-glucosamine constitute CS, a type of natural polysaccharide. Unlike other natural polysaccharides, CS possesses a positive charge due to a solitary amino group in the repeating glucosidic residue. CS is an outstanding polymer for fabricating nanocarriers because its reactive core amino groups can effectively interact with the host’s functional groups. [11] When the primary amino group is protonated at low pH, CS transforms into a water-soluble cationic polyelectrolyte, whereas it remains insoluble in water at neutral pH. Owing to its minimal toxicity, antimicrobial properties, and high biocompatibility, CS has been extensively evaluated for application in the preparation of drug delivery systems. [12] CS possesses strong hydrophilicity, which is attributed to its hydroxyl group, yet this polymer exhibits low mechanical strength. CS-based nanocomposites show potential in food packaging and biosensing due to their antimicrobial, antioxidant, and structural properties. They inhibit microbial growth, detect pesticides, and provide UV-blocking capabilities. [13–15].

Halloysite nanotubes (HNTs) are naturally occurring aluminosilicate clay minerals composed of layers of silicon, aluminum, and oxygen. [16] HNTs exhibit nanotubular structures with a diameter of approximately 100 nm and a length of around 500 nm. Due to the mismatch between the aluminum oxide and silicon dioxide layers, HNTs exhibit a helical twist. [17] One significant application of HNTs involves their utilization as a natural nanosorbent, either alone or in conjunction with other substances, to remove heavy metals. HNTs also find application in the preparation of drug delivery systems. Furthermore, HNTs can be integrated into polymer matrices due to their relatively high mechanical and thermal resistance and favorable compatibility. Additionally, they possess competitive pricing and can be employed as an alternative to multi-walled carbon nanotubes. [18] HNTs have been incorporated into thermoplastics like polypropylene and polyamides, improving their structural, optical, thermal, mechanical, viscoelastic, and dielectric properties, which makes these composites suitable for various industrial applications. [19,20] In shape memory polyurethanes (SMPUs) used for 4D printing applications, HNTs enhance mechanical properties such as tensile, flexural, and impact strengths, making the composite materials ideal for applications requiring repeated shape recovery and mechanical robustness. [20] Barium sulfate-coated HNTs added to poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) bone cement enhance its mechanical strength and opacity, providing better mechanical properties compared to traditional fillers, improved imaging contrast, and cytocompatibility for medical applications. [21] Quantum dots (QDs) are semiconducting crystals with a diameter of less than 100 nm in all dimensions. QDs are promising materials for various applications, including cell imaging, biosensors, drug release, and photodynamic therapy. [22] Research on nanocarriers fabricated from CS, HNTs, or graphene oxide and loaded with various cancer drugs has been documented. Some Examples include magnetic montmorillonite filled with CS for the controlled release of QC, [5] a composite consisting of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC), CS, and graphene oxide for the delivery of 5-fluorouracil, [23] CS-vanillin nanoparticles loaded with anti-cancer drugs for targeted delivery to the colon, [24] a CS/HNTs nanocomposite for delivering diclofenac, [25] and an HNTs/CS-based biological nanocomposite for the delivery of norfloxacin. [26] CS, HNTs, and GQDs, all selected for investigation in this study, offer distinct advantages to the QC nanocarrier. CS, a non-toxic, biodegradable, and biocompatible polymer, exhibits favorable compatibility with cells and tissues. [2] Moreover, its inherent mucoadhesive property and positively charged surface facilitate interaction with negatively charged molecules. GQDs dots functionalized with biopolymer CS exhibit enhanced stability and reactivity. Furthermore, coating halloysite nanotubes with a polymer shell effectively mitigates toxicity and provides a stable nanocarrier in the bloodstream. [27] Owing to the high surface-to-volume ratio of HNTs, the prepared carrier exhibits increased capacity for high drug loading. Additionally, GQDs have demonstrated safety in both in vitro and in vivo settings, further complementing their high surface-to-volume ratio. [28] Therefore, the combination of these three components can be deemed to provide a stable, biocompatible, and nearly safe nanocarrier in the bloodstream. Additionally, due to their unique electronic properties, graphene quantum dots exhibit self-emission upon light stimulation, making them suitable for drug tracking within the body. Halloysite nanotubes and CS are both pH-responsive materials, which contributes to the smartness of the drug delivery system. [29].

The innovation of our work lies in developing a novel hydrogel system based on Cs, HNTs, and GQDs to create a pH-responsive intelligent delivery system for quercetin delivery to lung cancer cells. Unlike existing systems, our hydrogel leverages the unique properties of each component. CS provides biocompatibility and biodegradability, HNTs offer high drug-loading capacity and controlled release due to their tubular structure, and GQDs enhance mechanical strength. Integrating these three components into a single hydrogel system represents a significant advancement over previous approaches that utilize these materials in isolation or simpler combinations. CS, GQDs, HNTs, CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC were characterized by FT-IR and XRD to study their functional group and crystal structure, respectively. The FE-SEM of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite was recorded to investigate the surface characteristics and morphology. DLS and ZP were employed to investigate the average size and surface charge of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC, respectively. Drug encapsulation and in vitro release of QC from CS/HNTs@QC and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC were also examined at two pHs (5.4 and 7.4). Additionally, MTT assay and flow cytometry were employed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of QC, CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC against L929 and A549 cancer cell lines.

The A549 and L929 cell lines were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC HTB-14, Manassas, VA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) with ≤10EU/mL endotoxin (Merck), CS (deacetylation >75 %, Merck), PEG 400(%99, Merck), acetic acid glacial 100 % (Merck), ethanol 96 %, Merck), SPAN80 ≥60 % (Sigma-Aldrich), graphene quantum dots (quantum yield >50 % and >0.005 % in H2O, Sigma-Aldrich) and HNTs with 30–70 nm outer diameter and 1–3 μm in length (Sigma Aldrich) were purchased. Dishes and culture flasks were procured from Corning Inc. Olive oil was obtained from Carlo Erba Reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel and loading of QC
ce:section.ce:para: First, a homogeneous gel containing 2 % CS was prepared by combining 0.4 g of CS and 20 mL of acetic acid (2 %) at 298 K under stirring. Then, 20 mg of halloysite nanotube powder was added to the hydrogel and dissolved in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. In this step, CS/HNTs was obtained. In the next step, 20 mg of GQDs was added to the CS/HNTs solution and stirred until it became completely homogeneous. Subsequently, QC was loaded into the prepared hydrogel (CS/HNTs/GQDs) so that the final QC concentration was 5 mg/mL. In the next step, SPAN 80 surfactant (2 % v/v) was added to 20 ml of CS/HNTs/GQDs loaded by QC using a syringe. SPAN 80, a non-ionic surfactant and emulsifier, stabilizes water-in-oil emulsions by reducing surface tension, preventing phase separation, and producing a uniform final product. It ensures stable emulsion, homogeneity, and effective drug delivery by dispersing nanocarriers in the hydrogel matrix. [30] The hydrogel coated with SPAN 80 was added dropwise to the hydrophobic phase. After 15 min, 20 mL of distilled water was added to wash the obtained product. The product was then undisturbed for 15 min to separate the aqueous (drug-containing nanocarrier) and organic phases. Finally, a centrifuge with 4500 rpm was used for 5 min to separate the phases, and a freeze dryer was employed to dry each sample. To prepare CS/GQDs, a homogeneous gel containing 2 % CS was prepared by dissolving 0.4 g of CS in 20 mL of acetic acid (2 %) at 298 K under stirring. In the next step, 20 mg of GQDs was added and stirred until a homogeneous solution was prepared. Schematic of CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel loaded with QC and preparation steps of CS/HNTs, CS/ GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel loaded by QC are presented in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of LE and EE of QC
ce:section.ce:para:
The loading efficiency (LE) and encapsulation efficiency (EE) of QC were determined to evaluate the impact of HNTs on the capacity of CS/GQDs hydrogel. Initially, 1 mL of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC was dissolved in 1 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) to prepare a homogeneous solution; then, 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added. The resulting dispersion was stirred vigorously to separate the ethyl acetate phase. The concentration of QC in the ethyl acetate phase was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 370 nm. Equations (1) and (2) were utilized to compute the EE and LE of QC (each experiment was repeated three times).
(1)
EE
%
=
Total QC
-
Free QC
Total QC
(2)
LE
%
=
Total QC
-
Free QC
Total nanocarrier

The average size of the nanocomposites was determined using dynamic light scattering (DLS), and the particles’ charge was evaluated utilizing a zeta potential (ZP) analyzer. Additionally, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CS/GQDs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC were recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using a PerkinElmer spectrum (10.03.06). The surface morphology of the nanocomposites was studied using field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (TESCAN) MIRA III model. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were acquired by an X-ray diffractometer (Philips PW 1730/10) to analyze the diffraction patterns of the CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposites and their constituents (2θ = 5°–80°, Cu Kα source with λ = 1.54056).

Dialysis was employed to investigate the release rate of QC from nanocomposites in laboratory conditions under two buffer environments, pH 5.4 and pH 7.4 at 37 °C [31]. These two pHs were selected based on the normal cells’ environment (pH 7.4) and the cancer cells’ environment (pH 5.4). The nanocomposites were transferred to a dialysis bag (MW 1200 g/mol), dissolved in 50 mL 20 % v/v ethanol /PBS (at pH 5.4 and 7.4), and immersed at 37 °C. Ethanol is used in buffer preparation due to the low solubility of quercetin in water, enabling accurate release experiments. To monitor the release of QC from CS/HNTs/GQDs hydrogel, 0.3 mL of the sample was replaced with freshly prepared PBS/ethanol (20 % by volume) at 0, 12, 18, 24, 48, 72, and 96 h. The amount of released QC was determined using UV–Vis spectrophotometry at 370 nm (measurements repeated three times):
(3)
Q C
r e l e a s e d
%
=
Q C
r e l
Q C
l o a d
× 100
Where [QC] is the concentration of
[ Q C ]
l o a d
in the nanocomposite (nanocarrier), and
[ Q C ]
r e l
is the concentration of QC released from the nanocomposite. Cumulative release calculations considered the withdrawn ethanol/PBS volume at each time point. The withdrawn solution (0.3 mL) was replaced with a fresh solution to maintain a constant concentration and simulate in vivo conditions, and drug concentration was calculated based on the total volume (50 mL). If the release rate is higher at pH 5.4, it suggests that the nanocomposite can be intelligently released in the environment of cancer cells and has minimal harm to normal cells. From these two environments, one can comprehend the nanocomposite (nanocarrier)’s response to pH and the sustained release of the drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetics release of drug in various pH grades
ce:section.ce:para: Using the Hixson-Crowell, Higuchi, first-order, and Korsmeyer-Peppas release kinetics models, the QC release data at pH 5.4 and 7.4 were fitted to elucidate the drug release mechanism. The parameters of each model were calculated using GraphPad 7 software, and the diffusion mechanism in both environments was determined based on the most suitable model.

The lung cancer cell lines A549 and L929 were also selected for cell culture studies. DMEM culture medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin (100 U/mL), and streptomycin (100 μg/mL) was employed for these investigations. An incubator maintained at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 was utilized.

In the study, the MTT method was employed to assess the cytotoxicity of free QC, CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC on lung cancer cell lines. A total of 28 tests were conducted in a 96-well plate containing a growth medium with
2 × 10
4
cells A549 cells. Cells were incubated at 37 °C and 5 % CO2 for 24 h to promote cell adhesion. After this time and the adherence of cells to 70 % confluence, treatment with the obtained samples of QC (
60
μ g
. m L
- 1
) was initiated. Control group cells were cultured under similar conditions (DMEM containing 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin) for the same duration. After 72 h, fresh DMEM (50 μL) and MTT solution (50 μL of 5 mg/mL) were added to each well, and the incubation of the plates was continued for 3–4 h. The media of each well was replaced by DMSO (150 μL), and the wells were shaken to solubilize the formazan crystals. A multiwell scanning spectrophotometer (ELISA reader) was used to record the optical densities at 570 nm. The measurements were repeated three times. The proportion of viable treated cells was expressed as a percentage relative to the control value (100 %).

To investigate cell necrosis or apoptosis induced by free QC and nanocomposites (CS/HNTs/GQDs or CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC groups), a double staining kit comprising Annexin V-FITC and PI was employed. Sample groups were treated with cancer cell lines at a 60 µg/mL concentration. Afterward, cells were washed with cold PBS, resuspended in 500 µL buffer, and stained with 5 µL Annexin V-FITC and 5 µL PI. Then, the dark-incubated cells were subjected to flow cytometry analysis for 15 min. The results indicated the ratio of viable cells, early apoptosis, necrosis, and late apoptosis. The above analysis was repeated three times.

FT-IR spectra of starting materials (CS, GQDs and HNTs), prepared nanocomposites (CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC), and nanocomposite loaded with QC were recorded to ensure the purity and successful fabrication of the nanocarrier (Fig. 1 A). As illustrated in Fig. 1A, the broad absorption peak observed in the range of 3286–3358 cm−1 is attributed to the overlapping stretching vibrations of the O–H and N–H bonds, which are characteristic of hydrogen-bonded hydroxyl and amine groups in CS. [32] The presence of –CH2- and –CH3 groups is indicated by a weak absorption peak around 2850 cm−1, corresponding to their symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations. [33] The peak at 1419 cm−1 is due to the bending vibrations of the –CH2 groups, while the peak at 1586 cm−1 is associated with the N–H bending vibrations of the amide II bands, reflecting the presence of residual N-acetyl groups. [34] Additionally, the peak at 1153 cm−1 indicates the stretching vibrations of the C-O-C bonds in the cyclotide structure, and the peak at 1056 cm−1 confirms the C-O bond vibrations within the etheric groups of the polymer. [35,36].

The functional groups in the synthesized GQDs were characterized using the FT-IR spectrum. The peak observed at 2928 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of C–H bonds, indicating the presence of alkyl groups. The band at 3447 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl (OH) groups, suggesting the presence of surface-bound hydroxyl functionalities in the GQDs sample. The absorption band at 1407 cm−1 confirms the presence of carboxyl (–COOH) stretching vibrations, indicative of carboxylic acid groups. Additionally, GQDs exhibit signals for the C-O-C stretching vibration at frequencies below 1192 cm−1, the C = O stretching vibration at 1725 cm−1, and the C = C stretching vibration at 1634 cm−1, which are characteristic of ester, carbonyl, and aromatic groups, respectively. [37].

Two distinct bands at 3694 cm−1 and 3621 cm−1 in the HNTs’ spectrum (Fig. 1A) are attributed to the inner-surface stretching vibrations of Al-O-OH groups. The notable peak at 918 cm−1 corresponds to the bending frequency of the hydroxyl (OH) bond, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups. Additionally, the peak at 1031 cm−1 characterizes the stretching mode of the Si–O–Si bonds, suggesting the presence of siloxane linkages. The Al-O-Si group vibrations are represented by a peak at 534 cm−1, which is indicative of the stretching vibrations of the aluminum-oxygen-silicon bonds. [38,39].

The FT-IR spectrum of CS/HNTs (Fig. 1A) reveals a prominent absorption band at 3439 cm−1, indicating the presence of hydroxyl groups in both CS and halloysite nanotubes. This band exhibits broadening and a shift in position compared to the individual components, likely due to the overlapping O–H stretching vibrations from CS and HNTs and possible hydrogen bonding interactions. The absorption at 2925 cm−1 is attributed to the C–H stretching vibrations, characteristic of the CS backbone. Additionally, the band observed at 1007 cm−1 is assigned to the C-N bond stretching in CS, confirming the presence of amino groups in the composite material.

The FT-IR spectrum of the CS/HNTs/GQDs sample (Fig. 1A) shows a distinctive peak at 3434 cm−1, which is attributed to the hydroxyl groups present in CS, GQDs, and HNTs. The two faint peaks at 2863 cm−1 indicate C–H stretching vibrations in CS. Additionally, the peak at 1636 cm−1 is associated with the bending vibrations of the NH bond in the amide groups of CS, reflecting the presence of residual N-acetyl groups. The prominent peak at 1067 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of the apical Si-O bond, indicating the presence of siloxane linkages in the HNTs.

The FT-IR spectrum of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC (Fig. 1A) displays a prominent peak at 3444 cm−1 and a weaker peak at 3439 cm−1, indicating the presence of phenolic hydroxyl groups in quercetin and hydroxyl groups in CS, GQDs, and HNTs, respectively. The absorption band at 2925 cm−1 is attributed to the C–H stretching vibrations in CS. The band at 1645 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibrations of N–H bonds in the amide groups of CS and the stretching vibrations of C = O bonds in the keto groups of QC. A distinct peak at 1050 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibrations of the apical Si-O bond in HNTs. The shift of the OH peak from 3433 cm−1 (in CS/HNTs/GQD) to 3444 cm−1 (in CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC) towards higher wavenumbers indicates the formation of hydrogen bonds between quercetin and hydroxyl or amine groups in the hydrogel (CS or HNTs). The shift of the C-O-C peak from 1067 to 1056 cm−1 suggests dipole–dipole interactions or hydrogen bonding between quercetin and ether groups in CS. These interactions contribute to the stability of the drug within the hydrogel, controlled release, and improved mechanical properties of the hydrogel.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were employed to investigate the components’ phase compositions and crystal structures by examining their respective XRD patterns. XRD spectra of the starting materials (CS, GQDs, and HNTs), prepared nanocomposites (CS/HNTs and CS/HNTs/GQDs), and QC-loaded nanocomposites were acquired and presented in Fig. 1B. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses of CS reveal a characteristic peak at 20.6° (Fig. 1B). [40] This peak is indicative of the semi-crystalline nature of CS, which typically shows broad peaks around 10° and 20° due to its partially ordered structure. A peak at 20.6° suggests that the material retains some degree of crystallinity, albeit limited, which is a common feature in CS samples. [41].

The XRD spectrum analysis of the GQDs reveals a significant diffraction peak at 2θ = 25.8° (Fig. 1B), which corresponds to the (002) plane [42]. This observation is crucial as it indicates the successful synthesis of GQDs. Notably, the absence of a peak at 2θ = 19°, typically associated with graphene oxide or graphite, further substantiates the formation of pure GQDs in the sample.

The XRD pattern of HNTs is also depicted in Fig. 1B. The characteristic peaks of pure HNTs are observed at 12.7° (001), 19.9° (020), 24.6° (004), and 39.6° (131), confirming their structural integrity [35]. The results are consistent with those reported in the literature [39,43].

The XRD spectrum analysis of CS/HNT nanocomposites reveals distinctive diffraction peaks spanning the 10°-20° range (Fig. 1B). These broad peaks signify the incorporation of CS and HNTs within an amorphous matrix. The loss of crystallinity in the HNTs can be attributed to the encapsulation by polymer chains, effectively altering their structural organization. Complementary SEM imaging corroborates this interpretation, demonstrating the successful integration and coating of HNTs with CS, as evidenced by their adherence and agglomeration patterns.

The XRD spectrum analysis of the CS/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite reveals a broad peak ranging from 10° to 30° (Fig. 1B). This broadening indicates hydrogen bonding and van der Waals interactions among the CS, HNTs, and GQDs, which contribute to the overall structural cohesion of the nanocomposite. The presence of these interactions supports the formation of an amorphous phase, as evidenced by the lack of sharp diffraction peaks typically associated with crystalline materials.

Incorporating QC into the CS/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite introduces a distinct diffraction peak within the 12° to 38° range, with a notable peak at 20° (Fig. 1B). This occurrence is likely due to the superposition of QC’s characteristic peaks (12.33°, 15.87°, 24.41°, 26.50°, and 27.40°) onto the existing CS/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite structure. The presence of these additional peaks confirms the successful integration of QC, further supported by the characteristic peaks of QC observed in prior studies [44–46].

DLS analysis is a technique used to determine the size and stability of nanocarriers. Additionally, ZP analysis is employed to evaluate the electric charge of particles. Based on the DLS measurements, the average size of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite (Fig. 2 ) is 225.6 nm, with most particles exhibiting a length of 279 nm. The measured ZP of + 48.9 mV indicates the nanocomposite’s remarkable stability. [47] The stability of nanoparticles is commonly assessed by their ZP, with values exceeding + 30 mV demonstrating resistance to aggregation.

The morphology and surface characteristics of CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite were investigated using FE-SEM analysis and are presented in Fig. 3 . The FE-SEM images reveal that the nanotubes become entangled after being coated with CS and embedded in the polymer network. This phenomenon is consistent with the findings of Lisuzzo et al. [48] in their studies of CS/HNT/khellin hybrids. In contrast, GQDs are uniformly distributed across the smooth CS surfaces throughout the hydrogel, indicating the absence of aggregation. The entanglement of nanotubes within the halloysite has resulted in the nanocomposite’s departure from its spherical shape. Nevertheless, DLS analysis and ZP measurements confirmed the nanocarrier’s favorable size and charge properties.

A dialysis study was also conducted to evaluate the release of QC from the CS/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite in two distinct buffer environments (pH = 7.4 and 5.4) at physiological temperature (37 °C) over four days. The prepared nanocomposite (CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC) possesses pH-responsive properties, which stem from the protonation of CS’s amine group, partial protonation of GQDs’ hydroxyl groups, and the resulting repulsion between the charged functional groups. [55] This repulsion triggers the hydrogel’s swelling and releases the encapsulated QC. To assess the impact of GQDs on the extent of protonation of CS’s amine groups in the nanocomposite (CS/HNTs@QC), the release rate was determined under both GQDs-free and GQDs-containing conditions. The results revealed that the QC release rates from the nanocomposite in the absence of GQDs at pH = 5.4 and pH = 7.4 were 73 % and 54 %, respectively, while these values increased to 97.5 % and 67 % upon GQDs incorporation (Fig. 4 ). This phenomenon suggests an enhanced protonation rate of the free amine group in CS upon GQDs addition. This can be attributed to the intensified interactions between GQDs’ hydroxyl groups, CS, and HNTs, augmented protonation of CS’s amine groups, and heightened repulsion between similarly charged groups in the acidic environment of the tumor, leading to a substantial rise in drug release.

Drug release data were analyzed using zero-order, Higuchi, Hixon-Kroll, and Korsmeier-Pepps equations to investigate the underlying drug release mechanism. The results are presented in Fig. 5 and Table 3 . Based on the R-squared values of the models employed to characterize the drug release data, the Hixson-Crowell model effectively describes the release profile at pH 5.4. This implies that in the acidic environment (pH of cancer cells), the repulsive forces between the nanocomposite constituents intensify post-protonation, stimulating drug release through dissolution. In this model, the drug release rate is primarily determined by the dissolution rate of drug particles, not diffusion. At neutral pH (7.4), both zero-order and first-order models exhibit comparable performance and outperform the others in their ability to correlate the release data. The zero-order model, which is independent of drug concentration, represents the behavior of drug release. If the data conform to the zero-order model, it suggests that drug dissolution is slow, and the drug concentration in the bloodstream remains relatively stable. The compatibility of the release data with the first-order model indicates that the release rate is concentration-dependent and that dissolution outpaces diffusion in the natural environment. Given that the release data in the biological medium (pH 7.4) align well with both models (zero-order and first-order), it can be inferred that slow dissolution is the primary mechanism governing drug release in the natural environment, with diffusion playing a less significant role.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of CS/HNTs, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposites against lung cancer cell lines A549 and L929 was investigated, and the results were compared with free QC. To ensure the accuracy of the tests, each experiment was repeated three times for all samples. Control groups, comprising the cell lines without added QC or nanocomposite, were maintained in medium (DMEM with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin/streptomycin) and exhibited 100 % cell viability after 24 h. The cytotoxicity results demonstrated that CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC exhibited the highest cytotoxicity, with a more profound impact on A549 cells than L929 cells. Based on Fig. 6 , the composite CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC demonstrates a potent and selective cytotoxic effect against A549 lung cancer cells while largely sparing L929 cells. The viability of A549 cells decreased significantly from 58 % with CS/HNTs/GQDs to 35 % with CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC, highlighting the synergistic impact of quercetin (QC) incorporation. Conversely, L929 cells maintained 94 % viability when exposed to CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC, indicating minimal off-target effects. Interestingly, free QC exhibited the highest toxicity towards L929 cells, whereas the nanocomposite formulation preferentially targeted A549 cells. [56] The enhanced efficacy against A549 cells in this study can be attributed to the pH-responsive nature of the CS/HNTs/GQDs nanocomposite, which likely facilitates controlled QC release and improved cellular uptake in the acidic tumor microenvironment. This targeted delivery system appears to amplify QC’s apoptotic effects specifically in cancer cells, potentially offering a more effective and safer therapeutic approach compared to free QC administration [57].

To evaluate the characteristics of the prepared nanocomposites and scrutinize the individual and combined influence of CS, HNTs, and GQDs on cytotoxicity, cell apoptosis was investigated in distinct groups (free QC, CS/HNTs/GQDs, and CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC). Based on the flow cytometry results presented in Fig. 7 , the CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite demonstrates superior efficacy in inducing apoptosis in A549 lung cancer cells compared to free quercetin (QC). The nanocomposite treatment resulted in a remarkable 79.55 % of cells undergoing apoptosis (74.4 % early and 5.15 % late apoptosis), significantly higher than the control and free QC groups. This enhanced apoptotic effect aligns with the previously observed cytotoxicity data from MTT assays. Notably, the nanocomposite reduced the viable cell population to 19.7 %, whereas free QC maintained 45.8 % viable cells, underscoring the nanocarrier’s ability to potentiate QC's anticancer activity. The lower percentage of necrotic cells in the nanocomposite group (0.708 %) compared to free QC (27.2 %) suggests a more controlled and sustained release of the drug from the nanocarrier, promoting apoptotic cell death over necrosis. This controlled release mechanism may contribute to the nanocomposite's enhanced therapeutic index by minimizing off-target effects associated with rapid drug release. The substantial increase in early apoptotic cells induced by CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC indicates its potential to trigger programmed cell death more effectively than free QC, possibly due to improved cellular uptake and targeted delivery facilitated by the nanocarrier system. [55,58] These findings collectively highlight the promising potential of the CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite as an advanced drug delivery platform for enhancing the anticancer efficacy of quercetin against lung cancer cells.

The research focuses on developing a pH-responsive nanocomposite for QC drug delivery using nanoemulsions containing HNTs, GQDs, and CS. The nanocomposite exhibited improved drug LE and EE after the addition of HNTs. QC release from the nanocomposite was evaluated in different pH environments, demonstrating enhanced release rates by adding GQDs. Mathematical models were applied to analyze drug release kinetics, revealing the Hixson-Crowell model suitable for pH 5.4. Cytotoxicity tests indicated higher cytotoxic effects on A549 cells with the CS/HNTs/GQDs@QC nanocomposite compared to other groups. Apoptosis studies supported the MTT results, showing more apoptotic cells in the nanocomposite group. Free QC exhibited higher percentages of living and necrotic cells, suggesting the controlled release of QC from the nanocomposite. Overall, the pH-responsive nanocomposite is promising to improve QC drug delivery and enhance cytotoxic effects on cancer cells.

Fateme Rezaei Abbas Abad: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Majid Abdouss: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
